attributes.stem:
A 33-year-old gravida 3 para 2 woman presents to the General Practice clinic at 36 weeks gestation with jaundice, which she has noticed over the last few days. She has experienced no abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fevers or changes to her bowels. She has experienced a mild itch affecting her hands and feet over the past few weeks, but has had an otherwise uneventful pregnancy. Her previous two pregnancies and deliveries were uncomplicated. She has no significant medical history and only takes a pregnancy multivitamin. She was born in Vietnam and moved to Australia at the age of five.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 110/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. She has scleral icterus. She has no visible rash. On abdominal examination, there is no tenderness on palpation. Murphy's sign is negative. The uterus is soft, non-tender and measures 36 cm, consistent with gestational age.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 110 g/L (115-165)

  • White cell count 6 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Platelet count 250 x 10^9/L (150-400)

  • Prothrombin time 11 seconds (9.5-13.5)

  • Total bilirubin 85 µmol/L (2-17)

  • Direct bilirubin 70 µmol/L (0-5)

  • Serum bile acids 19 µmol/L (0.3-10)

  • AST 50 U/L (12-38)

  • ALT 60 U/L (3-40)

  • ALP 300 U/L (30-120)

  • GGT 50 U/L (5-40)

  • HBsAg: Negative

  • Anti-HBc: Positive

  • Anti-HBs: Positive
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with pruritus, elevated bile acids, and elevated direct bilirubin levels. This clinical picture is most consistent with intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP). ICP typically occurs during the third trimester and is characterized by pruritus, particularly on the palms and soles, and elevated serum bile acids. Some patients may also develop jaundice, such as in this case.

ICP occurs most commonly during the 3rd trimester. If left untreated, complications such as fetal growth restriction, preterm birth or fetal death may occur. Definitive treatment involves induction of labour usually offered at 37 – 38 weeks gestation. The pruritus spontaneously resolves after delivery. Recent studies show that medical therapy with ursodeoxycholic acid is not effective in reducing adverse perinatal outcomes (PITCHES trial) and does not decrease maternal itch nor maternal bile acid concentrations, which is associated with an increased risk of preterm birth.

Primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) is a chronic autoimmune liver disease that primarily affects middle-aged women, associated with positive antimitochondrial antibodies. While it can cause pruritus and elevated liver enzymes, particularly a raised ALP, it would typically have a more gradual onset, and the patient's pattern of pruritus and elevated bile acids are characteristic of ICP. ALP is usually elevated in pregnancy due to production by the placenta. Chronic liver diseases do sometimes present for the first in pregnancy and should be considered alongside pregnancy-associated liver diseases.

Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but serious condition that usually presents with more severe, often non-specific symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and signs of liver failure. They are often hypertensive. Laboratory studies can reveal elevated aminotransferases and bilirubin, and grossly abnormal coagulation studies and biochemistry. The patient's relatively stable condition and the absence of coagulopathy make this diagnosis less likely.

Acute viral hepatitis can cause elevated liver enzymes and bilirubin in pregnancy. However, the hepatitis B serology indicating cleared infection (HBsAg negative, anti-HBc positive, anti-HBs positive) and the absence of other viral symptoms make this diagnosis unlikely.

HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, and Low Platelets) is a severe complication of pregnancy, usually associated with pre-eclampsia. The absence of hypertension and the normal platelet count make this diagnosis unlikely in this case.
learningPoints: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy typically presents in the third trimester with pruritus and elevated serum bile acids.
There are multiple potential causes of deranged liver function tests in pregnancy
references: Chappell L, Bell J, Smith A, Linsell L, Juszczak E, Dixon P et al. Ursodeoxycholic acid versus placebo in women with intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (PITCHES): a randomised controlled trial. The Lancet. 2019;394(10201):849-860.
Pataia V, Dixon PH, Williamson C. Pregnancy and bile acid disorders. Am J Physiol Gastrointest Liver Physiol 2017; 313:G1.

Nielsen JH, Lykke JA. Differentiated timing of induction for women with intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy-A historical cohort study. Acta Obstet Gynecol Scand. 2021 Feb;100(2):279-285. doi: 10.1111/aogs.13998. Epub 2020 Oct 18. PMID: 32970824.

Tranquilli AL, Dekker G, Magee L, Roberts J, Sibai BM, Steyn W, et al. The classification, diagnosis and management of the hypertensive disorders of pregnancy: A revised statement from the ISSHP. Pregnancy hypertension. 2014;4(2):97–104.
Ko H, Yoshida EM. Acute fatty liver of pregnancy. Can J Gastroenterol. 2006 Jan;20(1):25-30. PMC2538964.
